traps: Confusing the central angle (2θ) with the inscribed angle (θ) on the same arc.
Using the diameter in place of the radius in area/circumference formulas.
Forgetting that the radius to the point of tangency is perpendicular to the tangent.

subtopics.name: Quadrilaterals & polygons
subtopics.summary: Areas and angle properties of squares, rectangles, parallelograms, rhombi, trapezia, and regular polygons. Regularly tested, especially interior/exterior angles and diagonals.
formulas: rectangle: area = l·w, diagonal = √(l²+w²); square: area = a², diagonal = a√2
parallelogram: area = base·height; rhombus: area = ½·d1·d2, side = ½√(d1²+d2²)
trapezium: area = ½·(sum of parallel sides)·height; kite: area = ½·d1·d2
sum of interior angles of n-gon = (n−2)·180°; each interior angle of regular n-gon = (n−2)·180°/n
sum of exterior angles = 360°; each exterior angle of regular n-gon = 360°/n
number of diagonals = n(n−3)/2
keyIdeas: The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles; those of a rectangle are equal.
In a cyclic quadrilateral opposite angles are supplementary; Ptolemy: (product of diagonals) = sum of products of opposite sides.
Parallelogram law: d1² + d2² = 2(a² + b²).
For a regular polygon, work with the exterior angle (360°/n) — it is usually faster than the interior angle.

example.q: Each interior angle of a regular polygon is 156°. How many sides does it have?
example.solution: Exterior angle = 180° − 156° = 24°. Number of sides n = 360° / 24° = 15.

traps: Dropping the ½ in the rhombus/kite area (½·d1·d2).
Using (n−2)·180° for the sum of exterior angles (it is always 360°).
Assuming every parallelogram has equal or perpendicular diagonals (only rectangles/rhombi do).

subtopics.name: Coordinate geometry
subtopics.summary: Points, lines, slopes, and areas on the Cartesian plane. Sparse on CAT — usually one straightforward distance, section, or area question when it appears.
formulas: distance = √((x2−x1)² + (y2−y1)²)
midpoint = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2); section (ratio m:n internal) = ((mx2+nx1)/(m+n), (my2+ny1)/(m+n))
slope m = (y2−y1)/(x2−x1); line: y = mx + c
area of triangle = ½·|x1(y2−y3) + x2(y3−y1) + x3(y1−y2)|
distance from point (x0,y0) to line ax+by+c=0 = |ax0+by0+c| / √(a²+b²)
circle: (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r²
keyIdeas: Parallel lines have equal slopes; perpendicular lines have slopes whose product is −1.
Three points are collinear exactly when the triangle-area formula gives 0.
The centroid of a triangle is the average of its three vertices: ((x1+x2+x3)/3, (y1+y2+y3)/3).

example.q: Find the area of the triangle with vertices (2,3), (4,7), and (6,1).

traps: Forgetting the ½ or the absolute value in the triangle-area formula.
For perpendicular lines, using the negative slope instead of the negative reciprocal.

subtopics.name: Trigonometry (basic)
subtopics.summary: Right-triangle ratios and basic identities. Low-yield for CAT (roughly 0-1 questions, usually folded into a geometry problem) — far more relevant to exams like SSC/JEE than to CAT.
formulas: sin θ = opp/hyp, cos θ = adj/hyp, tan θ = opp/adj
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1; 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ; 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ
tan θ = sin θ/cos θ; sec θ = 1/cos θ; cosec θ = 1/sin θ; cot θ = 1/tan θ
standard values: sin30=½, cos30=√3/2, sin45=cos45=1/√2, sin60=√3/2, tan45=1, tan30=1/√3, tan60=√3
angle of elevation: tan(angle) = height / horizontal distance
keyIdeas: Memorize the 0°/30°/45°/60°/90° table — nearly every basic problem reduces to these.
Complementary-angle identity: sin(90°−θ) = cos θ and tan(90°−θ) = cot θ.
This subtopic is low priority for CAT; spend time here only after mastering the core geometry topics.

example.q: The angle of elevation of the sun is 30°. A vertical tower is 30 m tall. Find the length of its shadow.
example.solution: tan 30° = height / shadow → shadow = height / tan 30° = 30 / (1/√3) = 30√3 ≈ 51.96 m.

traps: Mixing up tan 30° (1/√3) with tan 60° (√3).
Confusing which side is opposite vs adjacent to the given angle.

subtopics.name: 2D & 3D mensuration
subtopics.summary: Areas of plane figures and surface areas/volumes of solids, including melting-and-recasting problems. A high-frequency, formula-driven CAT area where volume conservation is a recurring theme.
formulas: 2D: circle area = πr², circumference = 2πr; equilateral triangle area = (√3/4)·a²; trapezium area = ½·(a+b)·h
cube: volume = a³, surface = 6a², space diagonal = a√3
cuboid: volume = l·w·h, surface = 2(lw+wh+hl), space diagonal = √(l²+w²+h²)
cylinder: volume = πr²h, curved surface = 2πrh, total surface = 2πr(r+h)
cone: volume = ⅓·πr²h, slant l = √(r²+h²), curved surface = πrl, total surface = πr(r+l)
sphere: volume = 4/3·πr³, surface = 4πr²; hemisphere: volume = 2/3·πr³, total surface = 3πr²
keyIdeas: For equal radius r and height h=2r, cone : sphere : cylinder volumes are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3.
In melting/recasting or pouring problems, total volume is conserved — set volumes equal.
Distinguish curved (lateral) surface area from total surface area; the total adds the flat faces (bases).

example.q: A solid metal sphere of radius 6 cm is melted and recast into small cones, each of radius 2 cm and height 3 cm. How many complete cones are formed?
example.solution: Sphere volume = 4/3·π·6³ = 4/3·π·216 = 288π cm³. Each cone = ⅓·π·2²·3 = 4π cm³. Number of cones = 288π / 4π = 72.

traps: Confusing curved surface area with total surface area.
Using the slant height instead of the vertical height in the cone volume (⅓πr²h uses vertical height).
Forgetting the ⅓ factor for cones/pyramids or the 4/3 for a sphere.

subtopics.name: Divisibility & remainders
subtopics.summary: Rules and modular-arithmetic techniques for testing divisibility and finding remainders of large expressions. The single most tested Number System theme in CAT, especially remainders of large powers.
formulas: 3 | N (or 9 | N) iff it divides the digit sum of N
11 | N iff 11 divides (sum of odd-position digits − sum of even-position digits)
Divisibility by 4: last 2 digits; by 8: last 3 digits; by 25: last 2 digits
(a+b) mod n = ((a mod n)+(b mod n)) mod n; (a·b) mod n = ((a mod n)·(b mod n)) mod n
Fermat's Little Theorem: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for prime p, gcd(a,p)=1
Euler's Theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n)=1, where φ(n)=n·Π(1−1/p)
Wilson's Theorem: (p−1)! ≡ −1 (mod p) iff p is prime
(a^n + b^n) is divisible by (a+b) when n is odd; (a^n − b^n) is divisible by (a−b) for all n and by (a+b) when n is even
keyIdeas: Reduce the base modulo n FIRST, then handle the exponent — never expand the power.
Use Fermat/Euler to collapse the exponent: cut the power down modulo (p−1) or φ(n).
Use negative remainders (e.g. treat a ≡ −1) to make cycles trivial, e.g. any (n−1)^k mod n toggles between 1 and −1.
For a composite modulus, split via CRT (Chinese Remainder Theorem) into coprime prime-power moduli.

example.q: Find the remainder when 3^247 is divided by 17.

traps: Reducing the exponent modulo p instead of modulo (p−1) — Fermat cuts the EXPONENT by (p−1), not the base's power directly.
Applying Euler's theorem when gcd(a,n) ≠ 1 (it fails; handle common factors separately).
Forgetting that (a^n + b^n) divisibility by (a+b) needs n ODD, while (a^n − b^n) by (a−b) always holds.

subtopics.name: Factors & multiples
subtopics.summary: Counting and summing divisors of a number from its prime factorisation. A staple CAT topic — 'how many factors / how many even factors / how many perfect-square factors' questions appear almost every year.
formulas: If N = p^a · q^b · r^c, number of factors = (a+1)(b+1)(c+1)
Product of all factors = N^(d/2), where d = number of factors
Ways to write N as a product of two factors = d/2 if d is even, and (d+1)/2 if N is a perfect square
If N = 2^a · K with K odd: number of odd factors = number of factors of K; number of even factors = a · (factors of K)
Number of factors that are perfect squares = product of (⌊exponent/2⌋ + 1) over all primes
keyIdeas: A number is a perfect square iff every exponent in its prime factorisation is even — so it has an ODD number of factors.
For 'perfect square factors', count how many even exponents (0,2,4,...) each prime allows.
For 'factors divisible by m', factor out m first, then count factors of the quotient.
Number of ways to write N as product of two COPRIME factors = 2^(k−1), where k = number of distinct primes.

example.q: How many factors of 7200 are perfect squares?
example.solution: 7200 = 2^5 · 3^2 · 5^2. A perfect-square factor needs even exponents: for 2 choose from {0,2,4} (3 ways), for 3 from {0,2} (2 ways), for 5 from {0,2} (2 ways). Total = 3 × 2 × 2 = 12.

traps: Forgetting that exponent 0 (i.e. not including a prime, giving the factor 1) is a valid choice, so it's (a+1) options, not a.
Using d/2 for a perfect square N — its factor count d is odd, so the count of factor-pairs is (d+1)/2 (the square root pairs with itself).
Confusing 'sum of factors' with 'number of factors'.

subtopics.name: HCF & LCM
subtopics.summary: Highest common factor and least common multiple, plus their classic applications to remainders, fractions, and word problems. Steady CAT presence, often disguised inside application problems.
formulas: HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers (holds for exactly TWO numbers, not three or more)
HCF of fractions = HCF(numerators) / LCM(denominators)
LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators) / HCF(denominators)
Largest number dividing a, b, c leaving the SAME remainder = HCF(|a−b|, |b−c|, |c−a|)
Largest number dividing a, b, c leaving remainders r1, r2, r3 = HCF(a−r1, b−r2, c−r3)
Smallest number leaving remainder r when divided by each of d1, d2, ... = LCM(d1, d2, ...) + r
keyIdeas: HCF(a,b) always divides LCM(a,b), and both divide/are-divided-by the numbers — check answers for this consistency.
For 'same remainder, unknown remainder' problems, take pairwise differences to eliminate the remainder, then take HCF.
For repeating-event (bells/lights) problems, use LCM; for equal-grouping/tiling problems, use HCF.
Two numbers with HCF h can be written as h·m and h·n with gcd(m,n)=1; then LCM = h·m·n.

example.q: The HCF of two numbers is 12 and their LCM is 336. If one number is 48, what is the other?
example.solution: Product of the two numbers = HCF × LCM = 12 × 336 = 4032. Other number = 4032 / 48 = 84. (Check: HCF(48,84)=12 and LCM(48,84)=336.)

traps: Applying HCF × LCM = product of numbers to three or more numbers — it only works for two.
Swapping the fraction rules: HCF of fractions uses HCF of numerators over LCM of denominators (not the reverse).
Ignoring that the two numbers must both be multiples of the HCF and factors of the LCM.

subtopics.name: Primes & composites
subtopics.summary: Properties of prime numbers, primality testing, and structure of composites. Tested through counting problems and property-based reasoning rather than heavy computation.
formulas: N is prime iff it has no prime factor ≤ √N (only test primes up to the square root)
Every prime greater than 3 is of the form 6k ± 1 (converse not guaranteed)
There are exactly 25 primes below 100; 168 primes below 1000
1 is neither prime nor composite; 2 is the only even prime; 2 and 3 are the only consecutive primes
Number of composites from 1 to n = n − (primes up to n) − 1
Wilson's Theorem: p is prime iff (p−1)! ≡ −1 (mod p)
keyIdeas: To test primality, only trial-divide by primes up to √N (e.g. for N < 289 test 2,3,5,7,11,13).
Among any 3 numbers spaced by 2 (p, p+2, p+4), one is always divisible by 3 — useful for prime-triplet arguments.
Use the 6k±1 form to quickly reject candidates and to reason about differences of primes.
Any composite N ≤ 100 must have a prime factor ≤ √100 = 10.

example.q: For how many prime numbers p are p, p+2 and p+4 all prime?
example.solution: Among p, p+2, p+4 the three values leave remainders 0,1,2 in some order modulo 3, so exactly one of them is a multiple of 3. For all three to be prime, that multiple of 3 must itself be 3 — giving p = 3 (3, 5, 7). Any larger p forces one term to be a composite multiple of 3. So there is exactly ONE such p (namely 3).

traps: Treating 1 as prime, or forgetting 2 is prime (and the only even prime).
Assuming every number of the form 6k ± 1 is prime (e.g. 25 = 6·4+1 is composite).
Trial-dividing up to N/2 instead of stopping at √N (wastes time).

subtopics.name: Cyclicity & factorials
subtopics.summary: Last-digit cyclicity of powers and the highest power of a prime in n! (Legendre's formula), including trailing zeros of factorials. Both are recurring, high-yield CAT tools.
formulas: Period 2: 4→(4,6), 9→(9,1); constant last digit: 0,1,5,6
Last digit of a^n: reduce n mod 4, using 4 when the remainder is 0, then read the cycle
Highest power of prime p in n! = ⌊n/p⌋ + ⌊n/p²⌋ + ⌊n/p³⌋ + ... (Legendre's formula)
Number of trailing zeros in n! = highest power of 5 in n! = ⌊n/5⌋ + ⌊n/25⌋ + ⌊n/125⌋ + ...
Number of trailing zeros of n! in base b: take the minimum over each prime power in b of (its power in n! divided by its exponent in b)
keyIdeas: For last digit, only the units digit of the base and the exponent mod 4 matter.
Trailing zeros are limited by 5s, not 2s, because 2s are far more plentiful in n!.
For the highest power of a composite (e.g. 6 = 2·3) in n!, take the more restrictive prime (here 3).
For last TWO digits of a power, work modulo 100 using Euler (φ(100)=40) or cyclicity of the base mod 100.

example.q: How many trailing zeros does 100! have?

traps: Counting 2s instead of 5s for trailing zeros — the count of 5s is always the binding constraint.
Stopping Legendre's sum too early — keep adding ⌊n/p^k⌋ until the term becomes 0.
Using exponent-remainder 0 as index 0 in a cycle — a remainder of 0 means take the 4th (last) term of the cycle.

subtopics.name: Base systems
subtopics.summary: Representing numbers in bases other than 10, conversions, and base arithmetic. Appears occasionally in CAT, often blended with divisibility or digit-manipulation logic.
formulas: Value of (d_k … d_1 d_0) in base b = d_k·b^k + … + d_1·b + d_0 (in base 10)
Decimal → base b: divide repeatedly by b; the remainders read bottom-to-top give the digits
Last digit of N written in base b = N mod b; last two digits encode N mod b²
Number of digits of N in base b = ⌊log_b N⌋ + 1
In base b, each digit ranges 0 to b−1; carrying/borrowing uses groups of b (not 10)
In base b, the divisibility-by-(b−1) rule mirrors base-10's rule for 9: sum the digits
keyIdeas: Convert operands to base 10, compute, then convert the result back — usually faster than arithmetic within the base.
A digit d appearing in a base-b numeral requires b > d (e.g. a numeral containing 7 cannot be base 5 or less).
Trailing zeros of a number in base b are governed by the primes dividing b (same idea as trailing zeros of factorials).
Base-b palindromes and digit-reversal problems reduce to place-value equations in b.

example.q: Convert the decimal number 100 into base 7.
example.solution: Divide by 7 repeatedly: 100 = 7·14 + 2, 14 = 7·2 + 0, 2 = 7·0 + 2. Reading remainders bottom-to-top gives (202) in base 7. Check: 2·49 + 0·7 + 2 = 100.

traps: Reading the division remainders top-to-bottom instead of bottom-to-top (reverses the digits).
Using a digit ≥ b in a base-b number (invalid, e.g. writing 8 in base 7).
Carrying in tens during base-b addition instead of carrying in groups of b.

subtopics.name: Indices & surds
subtopics.summary: Laws of exponents, fractional/negative powers, and manipulation of surds (irrational roots). Underpins exponential equations and surd-comparison questions in CAT.
formulas: a^m · a^n = a^(m+n); a^m / a^n = a^(m−n); (a^m)^n = a^(mn)
a^(m/n) = (n-th root of a)^m = the n-th root of a^m
Rationalise: 1/(√a + √b) = (√a − √b)/(a − b)
If a^x = b^y = (ab)^z, then 1/z = 1/x + 1/y
keyIdeas: Reduce both sides to the same base to solve exponential equations, then equate exponents.
Factor out the smallest power (e.g. 3^(x−1)) to collapse sums of powers of the same base.
To compare surds, raise all to a common power (LCM of the roots' orders) so they share an index.
Rationalise denominators (and telescoping surd chains) using the conjugate to remove roots.

example.solution: Factor 3^(x−1): 3^(x−1)·(1 + 3²) = 90, so 3^(x−1)·10 = 90, giving 3^(x−1) = 9 = 3². Equate exponents: x − 1 = 2, so x = 3.

traps: Writing (a^m)^n as a^(m^n) instead of a^(mn).
Assuming √(a+b) = √a + √b, or that a^(−n) means a negative number (it means the reciprocal).
Comparing surds by comparing radicands with different indices without first equalising the root orders.

subtopics.name: Permutations & combinations
subtopics.summary: Counting arrangements (order matters) and selections (order doesn't) of objects. A core, high-frequency CAT QA area, usually 1-2 questions, often blended with probability.
formulas: nPr = n!/(n−r)!
nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!)
nCr = nC(n−r)
nC0 + nC1 + ... + nCn = 2^n
circular arrangements of n distinct = (n−1)!
arrangements of n items with repeats = n!/(p! q! r! ...)
selecting r from n with repetition allowed = (n+r−1)C r
keyIdeas: Decide arrangement (use P, order matters) vs selection (use C, order doesn't) before computing.
Multiplication principle for sequential independent choices; addition principle for mutually exclusive cases.
Handle 'together' constraints by gluing items into one block; use complementary counting (total − unwanted) for 'at least' conditions.
For identical objects divide out by the factorial of each repeated group.

example.q: A committee of 3 men and 2 women is to be formed from a group of 6 men and 5 women. In how many ways can this be done?
example.solution: Order does not matter, so use combinations. Choose 3 men from 6: 6C3 = 20. Choose 2 women from 5: 5C2 = 10. By the multiplication principle, total = 20 × 10 = 200 ways.

traps: Using nPr when order does not matter (or nCr when it does) — decide arrange vs select first.
Forgetting to divide by factorials for repeated/identical items (e.g. arranging LEADER = 6!/2! = 360, not 720).
Double counting in 'at least one' problems instead of using total minus the complement.

subtopics.name: Probability
subtopics.summary: Measuring the chance of events using favorable-over-total counting, built directly on P&C. High-frequency in CAT QA and often the payoff step of a counting problem.
formulas: P(A) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes
P(not A) = 1 − P(A)
P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A∩B)
P(A∩B) = P(A)·P(B) if A, B independent
P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B)
mutually exclusive: P(A∩B) = 0, so P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B)
keyIdeas: Count favorable and total outcomes with P&C; keep both counts on the same basis (both ordered or both unordered).
Use complementary probability 1 − P(not A) for 'at least one' type events.
Distinguish independent (multiply) from mutually exclusive (add) — they are not the same thing.
Use conditional probability when outcomes depend on a prior event or on drawing without replacement.

example.q: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum of the numbers shown is 8?
example.solution: Total outcomes = 6 × 6 = 36. Favorable pairs summing to 8: (2,6), (3,5), (4,4), (5,3), (6,2) = 5 outcomes. Probability = 5/36.

traps: Confusing independent events (multiply probabilities) with mutually exclusive events (add probabilities).
Mixing ordered and unordered counts between numerator and denominator.
Forgetting that without-replacement draws change the denominator (and use conditional probability).

subtopics.name: Set theory & Venn diagrams
subtopics.summary: Counting elements across overlapping groups using union/intersection formulas and Venn diagrams. A medium-frequency CAT QA topic, common as data-heavy 'survey' word problems.
formulas: neither / outside = Total − |A∪B∪C|
exactly two = (|A∩B| + |B∩C| + |A∩C|) − 3·|A∩B∩C|
at least two = (|A∩B| + |B∩C| + |A∩C|) − 2·|A∩B∩C|
keyIdeas: Apply inclusion-exclusion to avoid double counting overlaps.
For 3 sets, fill the Venn diagram from the innermost region (all three) outward.
Carefully separate 'exactly two' from 'at least two' from 'all three' — the pairwise counts include the triple overlap.
'Neither' means the total minus the union.

example.q: In a survey of 100 people, 72 like tea and 44 like coffee. If every person likes at least one of the two drinks, how many like both?
example.solution: Everyone likes at least one, so |T∪C| = 100. By inclusion-exclusion, |T∩C| = |T| + |C| − |T∪C| = 72 + 44 − 100 = 16. So 16 people like both.

traps: Adding group sizes without subtracting the overlap (double counting).
In 3-set problems, treating pairwise intersections as 'exactly two' when they also include the triple overlap.
Ignoring the 'neither' region when the totals are given.

subtopics.name: Binomial theorem
subtopics.summary: Expansion of (x+y)^n using binomial coefficients. Effectively not tested in modern CAT — a legacy syllabus item, so treat it as low-yield and study it only after the core topics.
formulas: (x+y)^n = Σ (k=0 to n) nCk x^(n−k) y^k
general term: T(r+1) = nCr x^(n−r) y^r
number of terms in expansion = n + 1
sum of all coefficients = put x = y = 1 → 2^n
keyIdeas: Use the general term T(r+1) = nCr x^(n−r) y^r to target a specific term or coefficient instead of full expansion.
Find a required power of x by solving the exponent equation for r, then substitute back.
Low priority for CAT — do not over-invest; a light familiarity is enough.

example.q: Find the coefficient of x^3 in the expansion of (2x + 3)^5.
example.solution: General term T(r+1) = 5Cr (2x)^(5−r) (3)^r. Power of x is 5−r; set 5−r = 3, so r = 2. Then T(3) = 5C2 · (2x)^3 · 3^2 = 10 · 8x^3 · 9 = 720 x^3. The coefficient is 720.

traps: Using term number r+1 versus index r inconsistently when locating a term.
Forgetting to raise the numerical coefficients (like 2 and 3 here) to their powers, not just the variable.

subtopics.name: Functions & graphs
subtopics.summary: Behavior of functions — composition, inverses, and graph transformations of modulus, quadratic, and step functions. A medium-frequency CAT QA topic, tested through max/min, symmetry, and function-equation questions.
formulas: composition: (f∘g)(x) = f(g(x))
even function: f(−x) = f(x) (symmetric about y-axis)
odd function: f(−x) = −f(x) (symmetric about origin)
inverse satisfies: f(f⁻¹(x)) = x
modulus: |x| = x if x ≥ 0, else −x
greatest integer [x] = largest integer ≤ x
vertex of y = ax² + bx + c at x = −b/(2a)
keyIdeas: Evaluate compositions from the inside out; domains must be compatible.
Use even/odd symmetry to shortcut evaluation and rule out options.
Read transformations off the graph: f(x)+c shifts up, f(x−c) shifts right, |f(x)| reflects the negative part upward.
Locate a quadratic's max/min at the vertex x = −b/(2a).

example.q: If f(x) = (x−1)/(x+1) for x ≠ −1, find f(f(x)).

traps: Computing g(f(x)) instead of f(g(x)) — composition order matters.
Ignoring domain restrictions (e.g. x = −1 or x = 0 excluded), leading to invalid values.
Mishandling modulus by dropping the negative-branch case.

scoring.note: Approximate, varies yearly with paper difficulty and slot normalization

iimCutoffs.topIIMs: 99+ percentile overall (GEM often 99.5+); sectional 80-90 percentile enforced
iimCutoffs.compositeScore: CAT + class 10/12 % + graduation % + work-ex + academic & gender diversity; then WAT + GD + PI

phases.name: Foundation
phases.focus: Concepts + fundamentals across all sections; build daily reading habit

phases.name: Practice
phases.focus: Topic-wise drilling, sectional tests, begin mocks

phases.name: Revision + Mocks
phases.focus: Full-length mocks + deep analysis, weak-area repair

mocks.cadence: 1 per 2 weeks early, 1-2 per week in final 2-3 months

sectionTips.varc: Read 5000-7000 words/day of dense non-fiction; RC is 70% so prioritize it; never skip VARC (sectional cutoff)
sectionTips.dilr: Set selection is everything: scan all sets first 2-3 min, pick the 3-4 most approachable; ~50 assisted then ~300 self-solved sets each of DI & LR
sectionTips.qa: Build Arithmetic + Algebra first (70% of section); master percentages/ratios early; shortcuts after concepts

commonMistakes: Quant-strong candidates under-preparing VARC and missing sectional cutoff
Chasing raw attempts over accuracy and set selection
